text: them downstairs, talking as they play cards. I can’t make out their words, but occasionally, laughter punctuates the low hum of idle chatter. I blink, and the sunlight in the bedroom has changed. I wonder how long I’ve been asleep. A swirl of pain runs through my stomach and I turn on my side, pull my knees up, and hold them close, like I’m stuck mid-cannonball on the way to a splashdown. I breathe slowly, and the pain subsides. I release a long exhale, stare at the ceiling and imagine a silver chain of bubbles rising from my mouth. Turning onto my back, my arms flop to my sides and I stretch my legs out straight. Minus the nails and crown of thorns, I’m Christ-like. I hear the creak of footsteps coming upstairs and I look at the slightly open bedroom door, waiting for Mom to ease it open and bring in a tray. But Dad is standing there peering at me, one hand against the door. When he sees my eyes are open, he gives me a thin smile and walks in. Today, he’s the deliverer of food to the leper colony, population one. I stare at the curls of steam rising from the bowl on the tray he’s carrying. Dad sets it down on my nightstand, puts his hand in his pockets, and shows me that paper-thin smile again. “How you feeling, kiddo?” I shrug and look at the bowl of soup. The steam reminds me of fresh chlorine powder tossed in the pool, and how it swirls in the water jet. Dad’s gaze remains on me and I can feel him waiting for a real answer to his question, but I don’t give him one. The spoon handle feels slick from the steam, and that bothers me, but I pick it up and take a big bite of the chicken noodle soup. “Glad your appetite’s still good, at least.” I swallow another spoonful. It doesn’t taste like it’s right out of the can. It’s been seasoned, too much salt, but also tastes of something bitter beneath the broth. “Mom make this?” I look at Dad, now waiting for a real answer from him. He nods, his papercut smile widening a little. “Made it special. We both did.” We hold eye contact for a moment, and though my eyes are wide open, I get the same uncomfortable feeling as if I was under water, like I’m stuck between worlds. Dad is the first to look away, mutters softly under his breath, “Well…” which is his way of saying he’s leaving. “Daddy?” He pauses, one hand on the doorknob, and turns back to me. “Yeah, Punkin’?” I stare at him, watch his face as I intentionally swallow another spoonful of soup. His expression gives away nothing, but the lids of his eyes are red-rimmed, like they were at Della’s funeral. I want to tell him it’s alright, and I understand. I want to hug him, though I don’t remember the last time we did that. I want to pat his back and console him like I’m the parent and he’s the child. I want to plant a kiss on his cheek that tastes salty like chicken soup, but also of something bitter and feral and invasive. I want to tell him I’m tired of waiting on the bottom of the pool. Instead, I speak softly. “I bet freedom tastes sweet.” Dad’s eyebrows shift slightly but do not knit together in a worried expression. He stares wordlessly, gives me a small nod, and then eases the door almost closed. I’m not sure if what I said was for me or for him, but I take another spoonful of soup, feel the noodles worm their way down to the molten lava of my stomach. The pieces of chicken are too chewy, but I swallow them anyway, and keep going until I lift the bowl with two hands and drain it to the very last drop. Quite simply put, Gwendolyn Kiste is one of the finest fantasists working today, her stories driven not only by a powerful sense of myth, but also a keen insight into the faults and frailties of human nature. Her novellas Pretty Marys All in A Row and The Invention of Ghosts are astounding works of supernatural dark fantasy which reinvent tropes for her own purposes, a skill she puts to marvelous effect in her short story collection, And Her Smile Will Untether The Universe. The title story of said collection left me breathless. I believe she will do the same for you, now. THE HAUNTED HOUSES SHE CALLS HER OWN by Gwendolyn Kiste H er first haunted house isn’t a house at all. It’s no more than a field, a grassy little spot near a Los Angeles housing development, the street shiny and new and filled with promise. It’s a Wednesday morning, a sunny morning, as bright as the smile she used to give her friends, used to give anybody who would give her a chance. That’s all she ever wanted: a real chance in the world. That’s the one thing she never got. A young mother with a baby carriage, out for an early stroll, discovers her. When she stumbles upon the crime scene, not knowing yet there’s been a crime at all, the mother thinks the corpse is only a mannequin, something plastic. Something fake. Then the truth settles over her, heavy as the California swelter, and a single piercing cry shatters the day. From there, it only takes an instant until they’re everywhere. The passersby and the policeman and the press that always follow close behind, their prying eyes never wanting to miss a thing. She’s a Jane Doe at first, but that doesn’t last long. Her fingerprints are on file, her mugshot more becoming than most girls’ screen tests. A few years back, she’d been arrested for underage drinking. Too much fun, the cops said.
